attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old man presents to the emergency department with an acutely painful right knee. He was playing football and tried to perform a sliding tackle, but collided with another player. The pain is a 8/10, constant and sharp in nature, with localised swelling and inability to weight bear on the injured leg. He has no significant medical history and has been taking protein powder supplements for the past two years to build muscle strength. On examination, his vital signs are within normal limits and his knee is tender to palpation anteriorly, with no erythema or bruising. He is also unable to extend his knee actively, and passive flexion and extension of the knee elicits pain.

X-ray of the right knee shows the patella located proximal to the femoral condyles and there is no fracture detected.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a patellar tendon rupture. This sort of injury is most commonly due to sudden contraction of the quadriceps to cause knee extension, such as performing a sliding tackle or high-impact jumping sports. Patients would be unable to extend their knee actively as the patellar tendon is connected to the patella, which is connected to the quadriceps tendon responsible for knee extension. X-ray of the knee would show a high-riding patella located superiorly to the distal femoral condyles due to unopposed quadriceps tendon force.

For a partial rupture, treatment mainly consists of immobilisation with physiotherapy along with analgesia. For a complete rupture, surgical management is required.

A patellar dislocation is a common sports injury especially in contact-sports, resulting in lateral dislocation of the patella. It presents similarly to a patellar tendon rupture, with anterior knee pain and tenderness and limited range of motion at the knee. However, there may also be buckling/locking of the knee as well as a palpable mass located lateral to the knee. In addition, X-ray of the knee would show lateral displacement of the patella rather than a high-riding patella.

Meniscal tears usually occur in young active patients with a history of traumatic injury involving rotational and axial loading forces on the knee. The medial meniscus is more commonly injured than the lateral meniscus, and clinical features include knee pain exacerbated by weight-bearing, joint line tenderness and restricted knee extension. McMurray's test can elicit pain and an audible click. The method of injury in this case mainly involves knee extension instead of a rotational force on the knee, and the knee X-ray clearly suggests a pathology that would result in superior translocation of the patella.

Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tears are most commonly associated with a direct blow to the knee or a a traumatic injury caused by a non contact pivoting mechanism. The acute nature and localised swelling is consistent with the patient's presentation, patients with ACL tears usually report a 'popping' sound upon injury and are still able to perform knee extension albeit with significant pain. ACL tears will have a positive Lachman test with excessive anterior translation, and X-rays would show a deep lateral sulcus sign or a tibial eminence avulsion sign (more common in paediatric age group) instead of superior patella translocation.

Quadriceps tendon ruptures are also a result of traumatic injury associated with sudden knee extension as seen in the stem, and also manifest similarly to a patellar tendon rupture with inability to perform knee extension and localised pain and swelling. However, the quadriceps tendon is attached to the superior aspect of the patella, and a complete rupture would result in a low-riding patella on X-ray, as well as a lack of quadriceps shadow. These injuries are usually treated surgically followed by physiotherapy for muscle rehabilitation.
learningPoints: A high-riding patella indicates a patellar tendon rupture, low-riding patella indicates a quadriceps tendon rupture.
Patellar tendon rupture results in inability to actively extend the knee due to disruption of the extensor mechanism.
references: Tarantino D, Palermi S, Sirico F, Corrado B. Achilles Tendon Rupture: Mechanisms of Injury, Principles of Rehabilitation and Return to Play. J Funct Morphol Kinesiol. 2020 Dec 17;5(4):95. doi: 10.3390/jfmk5040095. PMID: 33467310; PMCID: PMC7804867. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33467310/

Arnold EP, Sedgewick JA, Wortman RJ, Stamm MA, Mulcahey MK. Acute Quadriceps Tendon Rupture: Presentation, Diagnosis, and Management. JBJS Rev. 2022 Feb 7;10(2). doi: 10.2106/JBJS.RVW.21.00171. PMID: 35130193. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35130193/
